sections.title: See how a stack frontier turns local neighbors into one deep branch

featuredSetups.description: Use the same graph as BFS, then watch DFS follow the newest branch instead of finishing the whole shallow layer first.
Watch DFS go deep into one side of a cyclic graph, then backtrack when that branch runs out of new nodes.

paragraphs: Depth-first search keeps its frontier in stack order. The newest discovered node is expanded next, so the search usually follows one branch as far as it can before returning.

On this bench, keep the graph, frontier cue, and depth history visible together. That makes DFS easier to read: it is not random wandering, and it is not a shortest-path method. It is a branch-first rule applied step by step to the same graph that BFS explores layer by layer.

keyIdeas: Depth-first search expands the newest waiting node first.
DFS often increases the current depth quickly because it keeps following the active branch.
Backtracking begins when the current branch has no new neighbors, so the search returns to an older waiting node.

commonMisconception.myth: Depth-first search reaches the target fastest because it goes straight down one branch.
correction: Going deeper first is not the same as finding a short route. DFS can spend time on a long detour before it reaches an easier target on another branch.
The defining rule of DFS is stack order, not shortest-path efficiency.

workedExamples.title: Worked example: read the first DFS branch
workedExamples.intro: Use the frontier order and depth view together to read what DFS does immediately after the start.

items.prompt: On the layered campus graph, what does DFS do after A discovers both B and C?

steps.label: 1. Read which node is on top of the stack
3. Connect that move to later backtracking

applyAction.label: Open layered-campus DFS
applyAction.observationHint: Keep the frontier cue and depth graph visible together. Watch C go next because it is the newest frontier node, then notice how DFS stays with that branch until it must backtrack.

miniChallenge.prompt: Build a DFS case where the current depth rises quickly before the target is reached.
miniChallenge.prediction: Before switching presets, decide whether you need a graph with one clear branch or one that spreads evenly.
miniChallenge.answer: Choose a graph with a clear continuing branch so DFS can keep following the newest path for several steps.
miniChallenge.explanation: DFS goes deepest when each new node reveals another unvisited neighbor on the same branch before the search has to backtrack.

equations.meaning: A newly discovered neighbor is one edge deeper than the node that found it.
DFS removes the newest frontier node first, then places newly discovered neighbors on top of the stack.

variableLinks.description: Switch graph scenes so you can compare how DFS behaves on straight branches, hubs, and cycles.
Move the starting point and watch how the first DFS branch changes immediately.
Choose which node the branch-first search is trying to reach.
Use BFS as a contrast, but notice that DFS is the mode where the newest frontier node stays on top.

controls.description: Switch graph scenes and compare how the same DFS rule behaves on different connection patterns.
Choose where the traversal begins, then read which branch DFS takes first.
Mark the node the search is trying to reach.
Compare stack-based DFS with queue-based BFS on the same graph.

presets.description: A clear graph where the stack order and first deep branch are easy to follow.
A cyclic graph where DFS must avoid repeats and backtrack when a branch closes off.
A centered start where several choices appear, but DFS still commits to one newest branch next.
Use the same graph in BFS mode to compare branch-first behavior with layer-by-layer search.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep the current node's direct neighbors visible.
whatToNotice: DFS still makes each move from the current node's local neighbors, even when the overall path looks deep.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the branch-first behavior tied to actual edges instead of making it look like a mysterious path choice.

overlays.shortDescription: Show which discovered nodes are still waiting on the stack.
whatToNotice: The newest waiting node sits on top, so DFS keeps giving the current branch priority.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the stack rule visible on the graph instead of leaving it as a definition to memorize.

overlays.shortDescription: Mark which nodes have already been fully expanded.
whatToNotice: Visited marking stops DFS from reopening old branches when the graph contains cycles.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes repeat avoidance and clean backtracking visible at the same time.

graphs.description: Watch older alternatives remain on the frontier while DFS keeps one newer branch active.
Track how DFS often increases the current depth quickly, then drops when backtracking begins.
Compare new discoveries with repeat edges that visited state prevents from reopening.

noticePrompts.intro: Keep the graph and one history graph visible together, then watch which frontier node stays on top after each step.

items.text: DFS often increases the current depth quickly because the newest branch stays on top of the frontier.
Backtracking works because older alternatives stay on the frontier, and visited state stops the search from reopening already explored branches.

predictionMode.intro: Predict the next stack move before you advance the scene.

items.prompt: On the layered-campus DFS run from A, which node should leave the frontier next after A discovers both B and C?
items.scenarioLabel: After A discovers B and C

choices.label: C, because DFS keeps the newest discovered node on top of the stack.
B, because the oldest discovery always leaves first.
H, because the target always jumps to the front.

items.explanation: DFS uses last-in, first-out order, so the newest discovered node C is the next branch to expand.
items.observationHint: Run layered-campus DFS and watch the frontier cue after A expands. C goes next because it is on top of the stack.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the visible stack behavior and depth graph, not from memorized acronyms.

questions.prompt: Why does DFS usually feel deeper before it feels wider on this bench?

choices.label: Because the newest frontier node leaves first, so one branch keeps priority.
Because DFS deletes all the other frontier nodes immediately.
Because DFS ignores the graph's edges.

questions.explanation: The stack frontier gives one branch temporary priority, so the current depth often rises quickly.

questions.prompt: Which statement is most accurate about DFS on this bench?

choices.label: DFS is branch-first, but it does not guarantee the shallowest route to the target.
DFS always finds the shortest route because it moves quickly.
DFS removes the need for visited state.

questions.explanation: DFS chooses one branch first. That can be useful for exploration, but it is different from guaranteeing a shortest path.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one labeled graph with the current node, the stack frontier, and the visited nodes highlighted differently so the active branch stays easy to read.
A readout card reports the traversal mode, current node, frontier size, visited count, and target, while a cue panel shows the frontier order and the current neighbor list.
One graph tracks visited nodes against frontier size, a second tracks current depth against deepest reached depth, and a third compares new discoveries with repeat skips.
Together they show how DFS follows one branch, then backtracks when that branch runs out of new nodes.
